chunk_summary: This section outlines a tiered diagnostic approach for nonresponsive stunting and short stature, emphasizing secondary causes after nutrition-only management fails over 8–12 weeks, with specific tests and referral triggers based on phenotype and clinical red flags.
learning_objectives: Understand the criteria for transitioning from nutrition-only management to diagnostic evaluation in stunting cases.
Learn the initial diagnostic tests for nonresponsive stunting, including CBC, thyroid function, and coeliac screening.
Identify phenotype-specific triggers for advanced testing or specialist referral in cases of growth failure.
Recognize urgent escalation criteria, such as intracranial pathology symptoms or severe growth velocity decline.
key_facts: Nonresponsive stunting requires diagnostic evaluation after 8–12 weeks of adequate intake and morbidity control.
Initial tests include CBC, ESR/CRP, metabolic panel, thyroid function (TSH, free T4), and coeliac screening (total IgA and IgA tissue transglutaminase).
Bone age radiograph is recommended for markedly low height, delayed puberty, or endocrine concerns.
Phenotype-specific triggers include stool evaluation for chronic diarrhea and genetic testing for dysmorphism or disproportion.
Urgent referral is needed for intracranial pathology symptoms (e.g., headache, visual issues) or severe growth velocity decline.
reference_quote: ‘Bone age radiograph is useful when height is markedly low, puberty is delayed, or endocrine concern is high.’ (Reference 1)
document_key_topics: Urgent escalation criteria for growth failure
